topic: An athlete is asked to switch positions
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coach’s voice cut through the locker room, sharp and final. “Listen up, Marcus. We’re changing formations for the regional meet. You’re moving to the wing position.” I stared at him, my jaw tightening so hard my teeth ached. It felt like a deliberate dismissal of everything I’d worked for. I looked around at my teammates, some offering hesitant glances, others just looking away, and the heat rising in my chest was suffocating. I didn't say anything, just nodded once, a curt, stiff movement, and walked toward the changing room, every step feeling heavy with resentment.

[story 2]

"Marcus, we need you to take the center spot for the next game," the manager announced, his tone overly cheerful, as if suggesting a minor adjustment to a wardrobe. I felt a slow burn start deep in my gut. I stopped mid-stretch, my muscles locking up. I turned to face him, my expression carefully neutral, but the tightness around my eyes betrayed the churning feeling inside. "Excuse me?" My voice came out lower than intended, edged with a brittle quality. I took a slow breath, forcing myself to keep my hands still, refusing to let my shoulders slump in protest.

[story 3]

When the captain laid out the new lineup, naming me for the defensive flank, a cold wave washed over me. It was as if someone had just deliberately dampened the air around me. I felt a distinct clenching in my fists, a physical manifestation of the sharp prickle of being overlooked. I didn't look at the captain when he finished speaking; instead, I focused intently on the scuff marks on the floor, willing myself to remain utterly still, a silent, simmering frustration radiating off me like heat from hot metal.

I felt a slow burn start deep in my gut. I stopped mid-stretch, my muscles locking up. I turned to face him, my expression carefully neutral, but the tightness around my eyes betrayed the churning feeling inside. "Excuse me?" When the captain laid out the new lineup, naming me for the defensive flank, a cold wave washed over me. It was as if someone had just deliberately dampened the air around me. I didn't look at the captain when he finished speaking; instead, I focused intently on the scuff marks on the floor, willing myself to remain utterly still, a silent, simmering frustration radiating off me like heat from hot metal.
